text:
A wayside shrine, moss-eaten but swept clean by someone recent: a niche, a bowl, and the worn sigil of {god}, keeper of {god_domain}. The bowl holds two coins and a button. Standards around here are humble.

Nobody affected by "Forest Wayside Shrine" was consulted. The part authority avoids is that everyone agrees the land must be saved immediately after the next profitable quarter.

outcomes.text: You give three coins and a moment of actual quiet. Somewhere in the domain of {god_domain}, accounts are updated in your favor. You feel it land.
The coins settle. Nothing stirs. Either {god} is busy, or {god_domain} is having a quiet century. Faith includes the silences.
Two coins. From a god of {god_domain}. Bold. The road out of here feels subtly longer, and every door you pass creaks at you specifically.
You take them and leave the button, which is somehow worse. Nothing happens. Nothing happening is its own kind of dread.
A moth lands on the sigil, walks a full circle, and leaves. You will spend the rest of your life deciding what that meant. That's the sign.

text:
The temple of {god} dominates the square, all bells and incense and acolytes moving like clockwork. A donation plate the size of a shield waits at the door. {god} is {god_mood} this season, per the notice board. They post it like weather.

Power calls "City Grand Temple" an exception. The rule it conceals is simpler: a local grandmother has invited the haunting to tea and learned it takes three sugars.

outcomes.text: The plate rings. An acolyte anoints you with something that smells of {god_domain} and old bells. Blessed, certified, receipt available.
Generous, noted, and rewarded with the temple's hospitality: a meal, a bath, and the best sleep in three regions.

success.text: Your reading of the {god_domain} scriptures is heterodox, rigorous, and two hundred years overdue. They make you an honorary lector and pay a small stipend for the shock.

fail.text: You cite the wrong canticle. The silence has incense in it. You are escorted out with tremendous gentleness, which stings worse than force.

choices.text: Light a candle for someone gone

outcomes.text: One candle, one name said quietly, one coin. The temple noise recedes for exactly as long as it takes. Whatever gods are, this part works.

text:
Deep in the dark: an idol with the sigil of {god}, older than the temple style, older maybe than the name. Someone chained it to the wall facing the corner, like a punishment. The chain is on the WALL's side.

The official lesson of "Cave Forgotten Idol" is complicated; the useful lesson is that the demon has a mother, a lunch break, and a complaint about unsafe summoning circles.

success.text: Stone grinds. The idol faces the room again, and the air pressure changes like a held breath released after centuries. Something in the domain of {god_domain} is grateful, and gratitude from below is heavy coin.

fail.text: The idol doesn't move. The CHAIN moves. You leave at a pace you'll describe later as 'dignified.'

choices.text: Leave it exactly as you found it

outcomes.text: Whoever chained a god to face a corner had reasons, and you weren't there for them. You nod to the idol - hedging - and move on. Wisdom is mostly knowing which mysteries aren't yours.

success.text: Liturgical script, penitential grammar: 'UNTIL THE DEBT OF {god_domain} IS PAID.' Below, in newer scratches, a tally. The tally is almost full. You memorize the count and leave before it completes.

fail.text: The script swims. The one word you make out is 'SOON.' The dark feels attentive on the way out.

text:
A noise from your vehicle that vehicles should not make: part cough, part opinion. It rolls to a stop with theatrical timing, miles from anything, under a sky that's considering rain.

The official lesson of "Road Vehicle Breakdown" is complicated; the useful lesson is that freedom begins where someone can refuse an order without losing dinner.

outcomes.text: An hour of honest work and the kit's last good clamp. The engine turns over with what you'd swear is gratitude. You pat it. Everyone pats it. That's the rule.

success.text: Wire, sap, one sacrificed bootlace, and a theory. It holds. Mechanics would weep - some from horror, the good ones from respect.

fail.text: Your fix makes a new noise, WORSE, a noise with followers. You walk the last stretch pushing it, which is somehow more tiring than carrying it would be.

outcomes.text: A passing tinker fixes it in minutes for a fair price and one long, judgmental look at your maintenance habits.
The only passerby is a goblin scooter gang. They fix it FOR FREE, out of vehicular solidarity, then ride circles around you honking for a mile. The toll is dignity.

text:
The road ahead: empty, downhill, freshly dry, and gently curved in exactly the way that whispers to anyone with wheels. You know the whisper. Everyone with a vehicle knows the whisper.

The official lesson of "Road Joyride" is complicated; the useful lesson is that freedom begins where someone can refuse an order without losing dinner.

success.text: Wind, speed, one perfect drift through the curve, and a whoop you'll deny making. For ninety seconds the world is simple and you are its fastest citizen.

fail.text: The curve wins on a technicality involving gravel. You and the vehicle part ways briefly and reunite in a bush. Both of you are fine. Neither of you will mention it.

outcomes.text: You descend the perfect hill at a prudent pace, feeling the whisper fade into disappointment. Safe. Responsible. A tiny piece of you files a grievance.

choices.text: Close your eyes and trust providence

outcomes.text: Protected by providence, you surrender the controls. The descent that follows breaks no bones and three land-speed assumptions. You arrive blessed AND windswept, which is the best two-trait combination there is.

text:
A yard of vehicles in various states of hope: scooters, a wagon with 'RUNS GOOD' chalked on it, and one sky-metal thing under a tarp the dealer keeps glancing at. 'Everything's road-ready,' he says, road-readily.

Ask who pays for "City Vehicle Dealer" and the answer begins here: a full pantry and an empty street are conducting a quiet argument about distribution.

outcomes.text: It does, in fact, run good. The previous owner's lucky horseshoe is still nailed inside. The horizon just got closer.
It runs good for a day, then reveals its true nature: it runs ADEQUATE. Still, wheels are wheels, and the horizon doesn't check receipts.

choices.text: Ask about the thing under the tarp

outcomes.text: He pulls the tarp like a magician. Sky-metal, dune-rated, humming faintly with dead-corporation warranty energy. You buy it before your judgment reboots.
You kick tires with the gravity of an expert. The dealer matches your nods. Neither of you knows what the kicking is for. It's tradition. It's enough.
